questions.prompt: Value f(x) approaches as x approaches c.
questions.explanation: A limit lim(x->c) f(x) = L formalizes the value f targets as x approaches c. Newton (1665-1671 fluxion work) and Leibniz (1684 Acta Eruditorum paper) used intuitive limits informally; Cauchy gave the first rigorous definition in his 1821 Cours d'analyse, and Weierstrass refined it into the modern epsilon-delta form around 1859. AP Calculus AB Unit 1 tests one-sided limits, infinite limits, the limit laws, and the Squeeze Theorem. A crucial subtlety hammered on every AP FRQ rubric: the limit can exist even when f(c) is undefined, as in (x^2 - 1)/(x - 1) at x = 1.

questions.prompt: Limit as x approaches c only from below.
questions.explanation: lim(x->c-) f(x) considers only x < c, approaching c from the left. The two-sided limit exists if and only if both one-sided limits exist and are equal — a criterion first stated cleanly by Cauchy in 1821 and tightened by Weierstrass with epsilon-delta in the 1850s. Jump discontinuities are precisely the case of unequal one-sided limits. AP Calculus AB FRQs routinely give piecewise functions and demand students show lim(x->c-) f = lim(x->c+) f before concluding continuity, as required in 2014 AB 3 piecewise rubric — both one-sided computations must be shown explicitly.

questions.prompt: Form like 0/0 or infinity over infinity requiring further work.
questions.explanation: Indeterminate forms include 0/0, infinity/infinity, 0*infinity, infinity-infinity, 0^0, 1^infinity, and infinity^0. Cauchy catalogued the first two in 1821; L'Hopital had already published the 0/0 rule in 1696's Analyse des Infiniment Petits (using results Johann Bernoulli sent him in 1694). Worked example: lim(x->1) (x^2 - 1)/(x - 1) is 0/0, but factoring gives lim(x->1) (x+1) = 2. Critical AP FRQ trap: students must verify a limit is indeterminate before applying L'Hopital. Note 1/0 is NOT indeterminate — it is plus or minus infinity depending on signs.

questions.prompt: Theorem: continuous f on [a,b] hits every value between f(a) and f(b).
questions.explanation: IVT: if f is continuous on [a,b] and N lies between f(a) and f(b), there exists c in (a,b) with f(c) = N. Bernard Bolzano proved it in his 1817 Rein analytischer Beweis (first purely analytic proof, divorced from geometry); Cauchy gave an independent proof in his 1821 Cours d'analyse. Used to prove root existence: if f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, f has a zero in (a,b). AP Calculus AB FRQs (2007 AB 3, 2019 AB 4) require students to explicitly state continuity on [a,b] AND that N lies between endpoint values before invoking IVT — missing either hypothesis costs the justification point.

questions.prompt: f is this at c if lim x to c f(x) = f(c).
questions.explanation: Continuity at c requires three conditions: (1) f(c) is defined, (2) lim(x->c) f(x) exists, (3) the limit equals f(c). Cauchy gave the first formal definition in his 1821 Cours d'analyse; Weierstrass sharpened it with the epsilon-delta condition in the 1850s. Polynomials are continuous everywhere; rational functions are continuous except where the denominator vanishes. AP Calculus AB Unit 1 FRQ rubrics (2014 AB 3, 2017 AB 6) demand students explicitly verify all three conditions for full credit — writing only 'lim = f(c)' loses the existence point because conditions (1) and (2) are not separately documented.

questions.prompt: Discontinuity where limit exists but f(c) is undefined or different.
questions.explanation: A 'hole' in the graph: lim(x->c-) and lim(x->c+) both exist and agree, but f(c) is either undefined or different from the common limit. Worked example: f(x) = (x^2 - 1)/(x - 1) at x = 1 factors to (x+1) for x != 1, so the limit is 2 but f(1) is undefined. Redefining f(1) = 2 'removes' the discontinuity. Riemann (1854 Habilitation) and Weierstrass used such classifications to study pathological functions. AP Calculus AB FRQs frequently ask students to identify the type of discontinuity from a piecewise definition or graph, then justify removability.

questions.prompt: Discontinuity at a vertical asymptote.
choices: d y over dt equals k y
questions.explanation: At c, at least one one-sided limit of f is plus or minus infinity, producing a vertical asymptote. Worked example: f(x) = 1/(x - 2) at x = 2 has lim(x->2-) = -infinity and lim(x->2+) = +infinity. The discontinuity is non-removable. Cauchy distinguished finite from infinite limits in his 1821 Cours d'analyse. AP Calculus AB FRQs identify vertical asymptotes by finding zeros of the denominator that are NOT also zeros of the numerator — the latter give removable holes instead. On 2018 AB 5 students lost points for conflating the two types of discontinuity.

questions.prompt: Discontinuity with unequal finite one-sided limits.
questions.explanation: At c, lim(x->c-) f and lim(x->c+) f both exist and are finite but unequal, so the two-sided limit does not exist. Worked example: the signum function sgn(x) has lim(x->0-) = -1 and lim(x->0+) = +1. Common in piecewise functions, the floor function, and step-function physics models. Jump discontinuities are non-removable. Cauchy (1821) and Weierstrass classified these explicitly. AP Calculus AB Unit 1 FRQs (e.g., 2012 AB 4) use piecewise functions and ask students to test boundary continuity by computing both one-sided limits separately and comparing them to f(c).

questions.prompt: Required hypothesis shared by IVT, EVT, and MVT.
questions.explanation: The Intermediate Value Theorem (Bolzano 1817), Extreme Value Theorem (Weierstrass 1860s), and Mean Value Theorem (Lagrange 1797, rigorized by Cauchy 1823) all require f continuous on the CLOSED interval [a,b]. The MVT additionally needs differentiability on the OPEN interval (a,b). EVT guarantees an absolute maximum and minimum on the closed interval. AP Calculus AB rubrics (e.g., 2016 AB 4, 2021 AB 4) deduct points when students invoke these theorems without explicitly writing 'f is continuous on [a,b]' — the closed brackets and endpoint inclusion must be stated.

questions.prompt: Horizontal line y=L that f approaches at plus or minus infinity.
choices: a to the x times ln a
questions.explanation: y = L is a horizontal asymptote if lim(x-> +infinity) f = L or lim(x-> -infinity) f = L. For a rational p(x)/q(x): if deg p < deg q the HA is y = 0; if deg p = deg q the HA is the ratio of leading coefficients; if deg p > deg q there is no HA. Unlike vertical asymptotes, a function can cross its horizontal asymptote — Cauchy noted this in 1821. AP Calculus AB FRQs (2015 AB 4, 2019 AB 5) test end-behavior analysis by treating lim(x->infinity) f and lim(x->-infinity) f as separate computations. The asymptote describes long-run behavior, not local.

questions.prompt: Technique for rational limits as x goes to infinity.
questions.explanation: To evaluate lim(x->infinity) p(x)/q(x), divide numerator and denominator by x^n where n is the highest power in the denominator. Terms 1/x^k all go to 0 as x->infinity, leaving leading-coefficient ratios. Worked example: lim(x->infinity) (3x^2 + 5)/(2x^2 - 7). Divide by x^2: (3 + 5/x^2)/(2 - 7/x^2) -> 3/2. Cauchy formalized this algebraic manipulation in his 1821 Cours d'analyse. AP Calculus AB Unit 1 FRQ rubrics (2010 AB 4) award algebraic-manipulation points for showing the division step, not just stating the limit value.

questions.prompt: Behavior of e to the x as x approaches negative infinity.
questions.explanation: lim(x-> -infinity) e^x = 0 because e^x is positive and strictly decreasing for x < 0; conversely lim(x-> +infinity) e^x = +infinity. Euler popularized e in his 1748 Introductio in analysin infinitorum after Jacob Bernoulli first identified the constant in 1683 via continuous compound interest. So e^x has a horizontal asymptote y = 0 toward minus infinity but no HA toward plus infinity. The function is its own derivative — a property Cauchy emphasized in 1821. Used in exponential decay A = A0 e^(rt) with r < 0, appearing on every AP Calculus AB Unit 7 Newton's-Law-of-Cooling FRQ.

questions.prompt: Result when degree of numerator exceeds degree of denominator.
questions.explanation: If deg(numerator) > deg(denominator) in a rational function, lim(x->infinity) |f(x)| = infinity, so f has no horizontal asymptote. When deg(num) = deg(den) + 1 the function has a slant (oblique) asymptote, recoverable by polynomial long division. Worked example: (x^2 + 1)/(x - 1) = x + 1 + 2/(x-1), so the slant asymptote is y = x + 1. Cauchy treated such asymptotes geometrically in his 1826 Lecons. AP Calculus AB FRQs test end-behavior classification: distinguishing VA, HA, and slant asymptotes is required for Unit 5 graph-sketching problems.

questions.prompt: Growth-rate principle: e to the x dominates x to the n.
choices: 1 over square root of 1 minus x squared
questions.explanation: For any positive n, lim(x->infinity) x^n / e^x = 0 — exponentials grow faster than any polynomial. Cauchy proved this in 1821 using repeated L'Hopital. The growth hierarchy is logarithmic << polynomial << exponential << factorial. Worked example: lim(x->infinity) x^100 / e^x = 0 even though x^100 dominates for moderate x — the exponential overtakes eventually. Used in AP Calculus BC Unit 10 convergence tests; AP Calculus AB FRQ 5 from 2018 used this growth principle in a contextual limit problem about long-run behavior of a population model approaching equilibrium.

questions.prompt: Reason f(x) = x cubed minus x minus 1 has a root on [-1,2].
questions.explanation: f(-1) = -1 - (-1) - 1 = -1 < 0 and f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5 > 0. The polynomial is continuous on R, hence on [-1, 2]. Since f(-1) and f(2) have opposite signs and f is continuous, Bolzano's theorem (the IVT special case with N = 0, proved 1817) guarantees a root c in (-1, 2). One bisection step: f(0.5) = 0.125 - 0.5 - 1 = -1.375 < 0, narrowing the root to (0.5, 2). This is the canonical AP Calculus AB Unit 1 worked example, mirroring the structure of the 2019 AB 4 FRQ on root existence in a piecewise function.

questions.prompt: Iterative root-finding using repeated IVT applications.
questions.explanation: Bisection: starting from [a,b] with f(a) * f(b) < 0, compute the midpoint m = (a+b)/2 and evaluate f(m); replace whichever endpoint shares the sign of f(m). Each iteration halves the interval, so the error after n steps is at most (b-a)/2^n — linear convergence. Bolzano (1817) and Cauchy (1821) implicitly used the algorithm in their IVT proofs. Bisection is robust (always converges if hypotheses hold) but slower than Newton's method, which uses tangent-line linearization (AP Calculus AB Unit 4). The AP exam rarely tests bisection directly but the underlying logic powers FRQ existence arguments.

questions.prompt: f'(x) equals limit h to 0 of f(x+h) minus f(x) over h.
questions.explanation: The difference quotient (f(x+h) - f(x))/h represents the slope of the secant line through (x, f(x)) and (x+h, f(x+h)). Taking the limit as h->0 collapses the secant to the tangent: f'(x) = lim(h->0) (f(x+h) - f(x))/h. Newton (1665-1671 De methodis) and Leibniz (1684 Acta Eruditorum) discovered this independently; Cauchy gave the modern limit-based definition in his 1823 Resume. AP Calculus AB Unit 2 FRQs (e.g., 2017 AB 6) require this limit form to compute derivatives 'from the definition' — substitution alone earns zero of three rubric points without the explicit limit setup.

questions.prompt: Mathematician who introduced dy over dx notation.
questions.explanation: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz introduced dy/dx in his 1684 Acta Eruditorum paper 'Nova methodus pro maximis et minimis,' presenting calculus with differentials dy and dx as infinitesimals. Newton had developed his fluxion notation (dotted y for dy/dt) by 1671 but published later, sparking the bitter priority dispute that split British and Continental mathematics for a century. Leibniz's notation won because dy/dx makes the Chain Rule and substitution look algebraic. AP Calculus AB uses Leibniz (dy/dx, d^2y/dx^2) and Lagrange (f', f'') notation interchangeably; Cauchy (1821) standardized modern usage.

questions.prompt: Geometric interpretation of f prime at a.
choices: Some c gives f c equals average
questions.explanation: f'(a) is the slope of the tangent line to the graph of f at the point (a, f(a)) — geometrically, the limit of secant-line slopes as the second point approaches a. Newton (1665) called this the 'fluxion'; Leibniz (1684) called it the differential ratio. Cauchy (1823) gave the rigorous limit definition. The tangent line at (a, f(a)) is y = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a), the basis of linearization (Unit 4) and Newton's method. Every AP Calculus AB FRQ on tangent lines (2015 AB 5, 2018 AB 3) demands this point-slope form with f'(a) computed explicitly first.

questions.prompt: Condition for differentiability at c.
questions.explanation: f is differentiable at c iff lim(h->0) (f(c+h) - f(c))/h exists as a finite real number. Non-differentiability arises at corners (one-sided derivatives unequal, like |x| at 0), cusps (slope -> +-infinity), vertical tangents (slope -> infinity), or jumps (function discontinuous). Cauchy (1823) and Weierstrass (1872, with his nowhere-differentiable example) showed continuity does not imply differentiability. AP Calculus AB Unit 2 FRQs test corner detection: 2011 AB 4 used |x - 2|^(1/3) to probe whether students recognized vertical-tangent non-differentiability at the cusp point.

questions.prompt: Property differentiability implies but continuity does not imply.
questions.explanation: Differentiability implies continuity: if f'(c) exists, then lim(x->c) f(x) = f(c). The converse fails — f(x) = |x| is continuous at 0 but not differentiable there (corner gives unequal one-sided derivatives). Cauchy noted the one-way implication in 1823; Weierstrass constructed in 1872 a function continuous everywhere yet differentiable nowhere, dramatizing the gap. Contrapositively: if f is discontinuous at c, it cannot be differentiable there. AP Calculus AB Unit 2 rubrics (2010 AB 4, 2014 AB 3) require this implication: when proving differentiability fails, first check for continuity, then look for corners or cusps.

questions.prompt: d/dx of f times g.
choices: f prime g plus f g prime
questions.correctText: f prime g plus f g prime
questions.explanation: d/dx[f g] = f' g + f g' — the Product Rule. Leibniz discovered it in his 1675 manuscript and published it in 1684 Acta Eruditorum; the proof adds and subtracts f(x+h) g(x) in the numerator of the difference quotient. Worked example: d/dx[x^2 sin x] = 2x sin x + x^2 cos x. Critical AP Calculus AB FRQ trap: students sometimes write d/dx[fg] = f' g', which is wrong. The Product Rule appears starting Unit 2 onward; failing the canonical form costs the rubric point on every FRQ involving products. Cauchy gave the rigorous limit proof in 1823.

questions.prompt: Derivative of x times sin(x).
choices: sin x plus x cos x
f prime g plus f g prime
M over 1 plus A e to the negative kt
questions.correctText: sin x plus x cos x
questions.explanation: d/dx[x sin x]: by the Product Rule (Leibniz 1684) with f = x, g = sin x: f' = 1, g' = cos x, so the derivative is 1 * sin x + x * cos x = sin x + x cos x. This is a textbook example of mixed algebraic-trig differentiation. AP Calculus AB Unit 2 FRQs (e.g., 2008 AB 4) test this kind of combination: students must apply both the Product Rule and the trigonometric derivative d/dx[sin x] = cos x (proven by Cauchy 1821 using lim sin h / h = 1). A single sign error or omitted Product Rule term loses an FRQ point; sign care matters.

questions.prompt: Rule for d/dx of f over g.
choices: f prime g h plus f g prime h plus f g h prime
Square root of 1 plus f prime squared
questions.explanation: The Quotient Rule: d/dx[f/g] = (f' g - f g')/g^2, mnemonic 'low d-high minus high d-low, over low squared.' Derived from the Product Rule by writing f/g = f * g^(-1) and applying Chain Rule, or directly by Leibniz (1684). Worked example: d/dx[x/(x^2 + 1)] = (1 * (x^2 + 1) - x * 2x)/(x^2 + 1)^2 = (1 - x^2)/(x^2 + 1)^2. Sign order matters — many AP Calculus AB FRQ losses come from swapping f' g - f g' to f g' - f' g. Cauchy (1823) gave the formal limit proof; the rule generalizes to any quotient when g != 0.

questions.prompt: Technique using natural log before differentiating.
choices: sin x plus x cos x
f prime g h plus f g prime h plus f g h prime
questions.explanation: Logarithmic differentiation: take ln of both sides, use log laws to simplify, then differentiate implicitly using d/dx[ln y] = y'/y. Essential for y = x^x or products with many factors. Worked example: y = x^x, so ln y = x ln x. Differentiating: y'/y = ln x + 1, hence y' = x^x (ln x + 1). Johann Bernoulli introduced this method in 1697; Euler popularized it. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs (e.g., 2016 AB 2) use it for variable-base, variable-exponent expressions where neither Power Rule (constant exponent) nor exponential rule (constant base) applies directly to the differentiation.

questions.prompt: Generalization of Product Rule to three factors.
choices: f prime g h plus f g prime h plus f g h prime
sin x plus x cos x
questions.correctText: f prime g h plus f g prime h plus f g h prime
questions.explanation: d/dx[f g h] = f' g h + f g' h + f g h' — the Product Rule extends to any finite number of factors by induction, with one term per factor differentiated. Leibniz noted the pattern in his 1684 paper; it generalizes to Leibniz's formula for the nth derivative of a product, (fg)^(n) = sum binomial(n,k) f^(k) g^(n-k), a higher-order analogue. Worked example: d/dx[x sin x e^x] = sin x e^x + x cos x e^x + x sin x e^x = e^x (sin x + x cos x + x sin x). AP Calculus AB rarely tests three-factor products directly, but exam-style problems with composite expressions reward fluency.

questions.prompt: d/dx of sine x.
choices: x to the n plus 1 over n plus 1
questions.explanation: d/dx[sin x] = cos x. Cauchy derived this in his 1821 Cours d'analyse from the definition: (sin(x+h) - sin x)/h = sin x [(cos h - 1)/h] + cos x [sin h / h]. The first bracket -> 0 and the second -> 1 by the special trig limits (proven via Squeeze on unit-circle areas), giving cos x. The result is among the most-used derivatives in physics (simple harmonic motion) and on the AP Calculus AB exam. Every Unit 2 through Unit 8 FRQ involving trigonometric functions assumes instant recall — without it the cascade of Chain Rule, integration, and ODE applications stalls.

questions.prompt: d/dx of cosine x.
questions.explanation: d/dx[cos x] = -sin x. Proof via Cauchy's 1821 method using (cos(x+h) - cos x)/h and the angle-addition identity cos(x+h) = cos x cos h - sin x sin h, plus the trig limits lim (cos h - 1)/h = 0 and lim sin h / h = 1. The negative sign reflects the unit circle: as the angle increases through pi/2, cos transitions from positive to negative, so its slope is negative there. AP Calculus AB FRQ sign-loss is common: students write +sin x instead of -sin x. Memorize: derivatives of all co-functions (cos, csc, cot) carry minus signs.

questions.prompt: d/dx of tangent x.
questions.explanation: d/dx[tan x] = sec^2(x). Derived from the Quotient Rule with tan = sin/cos: derivative = (cos x * cos x - sin x * (-sin x))/cos^2 x = (cos^2 + sin^2)/cos^2 = 1/cos^2 = sec^2 x. Cauchy (1823) recorded this in his table of derivatives. The result equals 1 + tan^2(x) by the Pythagorean identity, an equivalent form. AP Calculus AB FRQs use d/dx[tan x] in slope-field problems and substitution integrals where sec^2 is the natural antiderivative target. Sign error or use of tan^2 instead of sec^2 is a common rubric loss on the 2019 AB FRQ 4.

questions.prompt: d/dx of secant x.
questions.explanation: d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x. Derived from sec = 1/cos using the Quotient Rule: -(-sin x)/cos^2 x = sin x/cos^2 x = (1/cos x)(sin x/cos x) = sec x tan x. Companion rules: d/dx[csc x] = -csc x cot x and d/dx[cot x] = -csc^2 x. Cauchy (1823 Resume des lecons) tabulated all six trig derivatives systematically. AP Calculus AB Unit 2 FRQs test these by composition: e.g., d/dx[sec(2x)] = 2 sec(2x) tan(2x) by the Chain Rule. Memorize the sign pattern: 'cosec' and 'cotan' derivatives carry minus signs; sec and tan do not. The 2018 AB 4 FRQ tested sec' inside a Chain Rule composition.

questions.prompt: d/dx of e to the x.
choices: a to the x times ln a
questions.explanation: d/dx[e^x] = e^x — the unique non-zero function equal to its own derivative. Euler proved this in his 1748 Introductio using the series e^x = sum x^k / k!, differentiating term-by-term. Equivalently from the limit definition: lim(h->0)(e^(x+h) - e^x)/h = e^x * lim(h->0)(e^h - 1)/h, and the second limit equals 1 by the definition of e. Cauchy (1821) provided rigor. This property makes e the natural base for exponential growth in AP Calculus AB Unit 7 ODEs dy/dt = ky, whose solution y = y0 e^(kt) appears on essentially every Unit 7 differential-equations FRQ.

questions.prompt: d/dx of natural log of x.
questions.explanation: d/dx[ln x] = 1/x for x > 0. Derived by implicit differentiation: y = ln x means e^y = x; differentiating both sides gives e^y * dy/dx = 1, so dy/dx = 1/e^y = 1/x. The Inverse Function Theorem (Lagrange 1797) makes this transparent: d/dx[ln x] = 1/(d/dy[e^y]) evaluated at y = ln x. Crucial AP Calculus AB Unit 6 corollary: integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C. The absolute value ensures the antiderivative is defined for x < 0 too. Forgetting |x| on an FRQ definite integral with negative bounds is a common rubric loss.

questions.prompt: d/dx of a to the x for positive constant a.
choices: a to the x times ln a
questions.correctText: a to the x times ln a
questions.explanation: d/dx[a^x] = a^x ln a for a > 0. Proof: rewrite a^x = e^(x ln a), then by the Chain Rule (Cauchy 1823) d/dx[e^(x ln a)] = e^(x ln a) * ln a = a^x ln a. The special case a = e gives ln e = 1, recovering d/dx[e^x] = e^x. Euler (1748 Introductio in analysin infinitorum) used this formula to study general exponentials and tabulate their properties. AP Calculus AB Unit 2 FRQs test it directly: e.g., d/dx[2^x] = 2^x ln 2 ≈ 0.693 * 2^x. Without the ln a factor the answer is wrong; the 2016 AB 4 rubric deducted points for omission of this multiplier on the differentiation step.

questions.prompt: d/dx of log base a of x.
choices: a to the x times ln a
questions.explanation: d/dx[log_a x] = 1/(x ln a) for x > 0, a > 0, a != 1. Derived via the change-of-base formula log_a x = ln x / ln a (Euler 1748 Introductio), so d/dx[log_a x] = (1/ln a) * d/dx[ln x] = 1/(x ln a). The special case a = e gives ln a = 1 and recovers d/dx[ln x] = 1/x. For a = 10, the result is 1/(x ln 10) ≈ 0.434/x. AP Calculus AB rarely uses non-natural logs; when it does (typically a contextual finance compound-interest or pH problem), the change-of-base formula is the cleanest path through the differentiation step on the FRQ rubric without invoking implicit differentiation.

questions.prompt: Method used when both base and exponent are variable.
questions.explanation: For y = u(x)^v(x) (both base and exponent variable), neither Power Rule (constant exponent) nor exponential rule (constant base) applies. Take ln: ln y = v(x) ln u(x). Differentiate implicitly: y'/y = v' ln u + v u'/u, so y' = u^v (v' ln u + v u'/u). Worked example: y = x^x. ln y = x ln x, so y'/y = ln x + 1, hence y' = x^x (ln x + 1). Johann Bernoulli (1697) introduced the technique. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs (e.g., 2016 AB 2) test logarithmic differentiation on x^x and (sin x)^x type problems; it is also the cleanest route to differentiating long products with many factors.

questions.prompt: Rule for d/dx of f(g(x)).
questions.explanation: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) * g'(x) — the Chain Rule. Leibniz (1676 manuscript) used it implicitly when manipulating differentials dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx); Cauchy (1823 Resume) gave the rigorous modern statement and proof from the limit definition. The rule encodes the principle that derivatives compose multiplicatively along a chain of dependencies. Worked example: d/dx[(2x + 1)^5] = 5(2x + 1)^4 * 2 = 10(2x + 1)^4. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs treat the Chain Rule as the central skill; nearly every derivative problem from Unit 2 onward requires it implicitly via composition.

questions.prompt: d/dx of (3x plus 1) to the fifth.
questions.explanation: d/dx[(3x + 1)^5]: by the Chain Rule (Leibniz 1676, Cauchy 1823), differentiate the outer power (n = 5) to get 5(3x + 1)^4, then multiply by the inner derivative d/dx[3x + 1] = 3. Result: 5 * 3 * (3x + 1)^4 = 15(3x + 1)^4. This is the canonical AP Calculus AB Unit 3 illustration of Chain Rule with a linear inner function. Common FRQ error: students compute 5(3x + 1)^4 and forget the inner-derivative factor of 3. The 2015 AB 4 rubric awarded a separate point for the inner-derivative multiplication.

questions.prompt: d/dx of sin of x squared.
questions.explanation: d/dx[sin(x^2)]: outer function sin -> cos, evaluated at x^2; inner derivative d/dx[x^2] = 2x. Chain Rule (Leibniz 1676 manuscript, Cauchy 1823) gives cos(x^2) * 2x = 2x cos(x^2). Crucial AP Calculus AB Unit 3 subtlety: sin(x^2) is NOT the same as sin^2(x). The former is sin composed with x^2 (with derivative 2x cos(x^2)); the latter is (sin x)^2 and differentiates to 2 sin x cos x = sin(2x) by Chain Rule with outer u^2. Notation reading is half the battle. FRQ 2018 AB 4 explicitly tested this distinction, deducting points when students conflated sin(x^2) and sin^2(x).

questions.prompt: d/dx of e to the 3x.
questions.explanation: d/dx[e^(3x)]: outer derivative d/du[e^u] = e^u evaluated at u = 3x gives e^(3x); inner derivative d/dx[3x] = 3. Chain Rule (Cauchy 1823): e^(3x) * 3 = 3 e^(3x). More generally d/dx[e^(kx)] = k e^(kx), making e^(kx) the eigenfunction of d/dx with eigenvalue k — the analytic backbone of every AP Calculus AB Unit 7 ODE dy/dt = ky, whose solution y = y_0 e^(kt) is recovered by separation. Forgetting the factor of k is the most common FRQ error in Unit 7 exponential models; the 2014 AB 6 rubric required the k-multiplier to award full credit on the differentiation step.

questions.prompt: d/dx of natural log of f(x).
questions.explanation: d/dx[ln(f(x))] = f'(x)/f(x), by Chain Rule with outer ln (derivative 1/u) and inner f. Worked example: d/dx[ln(sin x)] = cos x / sin x = cot x. The expression f'/f is called the logarithmic derivative; it converts multiplicative structure into additive via d/dx[ln(fg)] = f'/f + g'/g, the engine of logarithmic differentiation (Johann Bernoulli 1697). AP Calculus AB Unit 6 corollary: integral of f'/f dx = ln|f(x)| + C, used heavily in u-substitution where u = f. The 2017 AB 6 FRQ used this pattern in disguised form.

questions.prompt: Technique used when y cannot be solved for in terms of x.
choices: d squared y over d x squared
y is implicitly a function of x
questions.explanation: Implicit differentiation: differentiate both sides of F(x, y) = 0 with respect to x, treating y as a function of x (so d/dx[y^n] = n y^(n-1) y' by Chain Rule), then solve for y' = dy/dx. Leibniz (1684) used the technique informally; Cauchy (1823) systematized it; the modern statement rests on the Implicit Function Theorem of Dini (1877). Necessary when y cannot be isolated, as in x^2 + y^2 = 25 or e^y + x y = 1. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs (2008 AB 6, 2015 AB 6) test implicit differentiation on curves where explicit solution is algebraically impossible.

questions.prompt: dy over dx for x squared plus y squared equals 25.
choices: f g(x) times g prime x
y is implicitly a function of x
questions.explanation: x^2 + y^2 = 25 (circle of radius 5). Differentiate implicitly: 2x + 2y dy/dx = 0 (using Chain Rule on y^2 to get 2y * y'). Solve: dy/dx = -x/y. The result depends on both x and y, which is correct because the circle has two y-values for each x in (-5, 5), each with opposite slope. Geometric check: the radius vector (x, y) and the tangent are perpendicular, so the tangent slope is -x/y. Cauchy (1823) used the circle as the standard implicit example. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs frequently use conic sections to probe this skill.

questions.prompt: Method for finding tangent on an implicitly defined curve.
choices: y is implicitly a function of x
questions.explanation: Implicit differentiation gives dy/dx as a function of (x, y); evaluating at a point on the curve yields the slope, and the tangent line is y - y0 = m(x - x0). Worked example: tangent to x^3 + y^3 = 9 at (1, 2). Implicit: 3x^2 + 3y^2 y' = 0, so y' = -x^2/y^2 = -1/4 at (1, 2). Tangent: y = 2 - (1/4)(x - 1). Leibniz (1684) introduced the technique; Cauchy (1823) made it rigorous. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs (2008 AB 6, 2015 AB 6) demand explicit setup of the implicit derivative, substitution of (x0, y0), then point-slope form.

questions.prompt: d/dx of arcsine x.
choices: f g(x) times g prime x
1 over square root of 1 minus x squared
negative 1 over square root of 1 minus x squared
questions.correctText: 1 over square root of 1 minus x squared
questions.explanation: d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/sqrt(1 - x^2) for x in (-1, 1). Derived from the Inverse Function Theorem (Lagrange 1797 Theorie des fonctions analytiques, Cauchy 1823 Resume): if y = arcsin x then sin y = x. Differentiating both sides: cos y * dy/dx = 1, so dy/dx = 1/cos y. Use cos y = sqrt(1 - sin^2 y) = sqrt(1 - x^2) by Pythagorean identity. The domain restriction y in [-pi/2, pi/2] ensures cos y >= 0, so the positive root is correct. Crucial Unit 6 antiderivative: integral 1/sqrt(1 - x^2) dx = arcsin x + C. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs (2017 AB 5) integrate this into Chain Rule compositions like d/dx[arcsin(x^2)].

questions.prompt: d/dx of arctangent x.
choices: negative 1 over square root of 1 minus x squared
questions.explanation: d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1 + x^2) for all real x. Derived from Inverse Function Theorem (Lagrange 1797): y = arctan x means tan y = x. Differentiating: sec^2 y * dy/dx = 1, so dy/dx = 1/sec^2 y = cos^2 y. Use 1 + tan^2 y = sec^2 y, so cos^2 y = 1/(1 + tan^2 y) = 1/(1 + x^2). This derivative is defined everywhere — arctan has no domain restriction other than the principal branch. Crucial Unit 6 antiderivative: integral of 1/(1 + x^2) dx = arctan x + C, appearing on AP Calculus AB FRQ 2014 AB 6 substitution problems.

questions.prompt: Theorem giving derivative of inverse function.
choices: f prime g plus f g prime
questions.explanation: The Inverse Function Theorem: if f is differentiable at a with f'(a) != 0, then f^(-1) exists locally near b = f(a) and (f^(-1))'(b) = 1/f'(a). Lagrange stated it in his 1770 Reversion of Series work and his 1797 Theorie des fonctions; Cauchy (1823) gave the rigorous limit proof. The theorem is the engine behind d/dx[arcsin x], d/dx[ln x], and every inverse derivative. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs (e.g., 2008 AB 4, 2014 AB 3) test it numerically: 'if f(2) = 5 and f'(2) = 3, find (f^(-1))'(5).' Answer: 1/3.

questions.prompt: d/dx of arccosine x.
choices: negative 1 over square root of 1 minus x squared
questions.correctText: negative 1 over square root of 1 minus x squared
questions.explanation: d/dx[arccos x] = -1/sqrt(1 - x^2) for x in (-1, 1). Derived analogously to arcsin: y = arccos x means cos y = x, so -sin y * dy/dx = 1, giving dy/dx = -1/sin y = -1/sqrt(1 - x^2). The minus sign reflects that arccos is decreasing on [-1, 1]. Note the identity arcsin x + arccos x = pi/2 (Cauchy 1823) implies their derivatives sum to 0, an elegant cross-check. AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs sometimes test arccos in disguised form via integral of -1/sqrt(1 - x^2) dx = arccos x + C, an antiderivative pattern.

questions.prompt: Slope of tangent to f inverse at y equals 4 if f prime of 2 equals 3 and f(2) equals 4.
choices: negative 1 over square root of 1 minus x squared
questions.explanation: By the Inverse Function Theorem (Lagrange 1770, 1797; Cauchy 1823): if f(2) = 4 and f'(2) = 3, then (f^(-1))'(4) = 1/f'(2) = 1/3. The relationship is geometric: graphs of f and f^(-1) are reflections across y = x, so a tangent line at (2, 4) on f with slope 3 reflects to a tangent at (4, 2) on f^(-1) with slope 1/3 (reciprocal of the original slope). AP Calculus AB Unit 3 FRQs test this numerically every few years (2008 AB 4 was the classic example), giving f(a) and f'(a) and asking for (f^(-1))'(f(a)).

questions.prompt: Notation for the second derivative.
questions.explanation: f''(x) — Lagrange notation for the second derivative — is the derivative of f'(x), so f''(x) = d/dx[f'(x)] = d^2 f / dx^2 (Leibniz notation). Lagrange introduced the prime notation in his 1797 Theorie des fonctions analytiques; Leibniz used d^2 y/dx^2 from 1675. Geometrically, f'' measures the rate of change of slope, which AP Calculus AB Unit 5 connects to concavity: f'' > 0 means concave up, f'' < 0 means concave down. Physically, when f represents position, f' is velocity and f'' is acceleration, the central Unit 4 application of higher-order differentiation.

questions.prompt: Physical interpretation of position's second derivative.
choices: k to the n times e to the k x
dQ over dt equals rate in minus rate out
questions.explanation: If s(t) is position, then s'(t) = v(t) is velocity and s''(t) = a(t) is acceleration. Newton (1687 Principia) used fluxions to articulate this exact chain in his laws of motion; Leibniz used differentials in parallel. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, a measurement Newton's Second Law connects to force F = m a. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs on particle motion (e.g., 2013 AB 4, 2017 AB 5) demand fluent reading of these three layers: identify position, velocity, and acceleration; identify when the particle is speeding up (v and a have the same sign, a Unit 4 rubric item).

questions.prompt: Determination of concavity from the second derivative.
choices: dQ over dt equals rate in minus rate out
questions.explanation: Concavity is determined by the sign of f''(x): f'' > 0 on an interval means f is concave up (graph curves upward, like a cup); f'' < 0 means concave down. Where f'' changes sign and f is continuous, f has an inflection point. The Second Derivative Test (a corollary): at a critical point c, if f''(c) > 0 then c is a local min; if f''(c) < 0 then c is a local max; if f''(c) = 0 the test is inconclusive. Cauchy (1829) formalized concavity in terms of f''. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2013 AB 4, 2018 AB 5) require sign-chart justifications for concavity intervals.

questions.prompt: d squared over dx squared of sin x.
choices: k to the n times e to the k x
questions.explanation: d^2/dx^2[sin x] = -sin x. First derivative: d/dx[sin x] = cos x (Cauchy 1821 via Squeeze + difference quotient). Second derivative: d/dx[cos x] = -sin x. The fourth derivative returns to sin x — sin is its own fourth derivative, an eigenfunction of d^4/dx^4 with eigenvalue 1. The differential equation y'' + y = 0 (the simple-harmonic-motion equation, Newton 1687) has sin and cos as a basis of solutions, a fact exploited in AP Calculus BC Unit 9 series and AP Calculus AB Unit 7 ODE classification problems.

questions.prompt: Pattern in nth derivative of e to the k x.
choices: k to the n times e to the k x
questions.correctText: k to the n times e to the k x
questions.explanation: d^n/dx^n[e^(kx)] = k^n e^(kx) — each differentiation produces a factor of k via the Chain Rule, accumulating to k^n. Equivalently, e^(kx) is an eigenfunction of d^n/dx^n with eigenvalue k^n. Worked example: d^3/dx^3[e^(2x)] = 8 e^(2x), since 2^3 = 8. Euler (1748) used these eigenvalue relations to solve linear ODEs with constant coefficients via the characteristic equation. AP Calculus AB Unit 7 FRQs implicitly use this when solving dy/dt = k y; the eigenfunction structure is also pivotal for AP Calculus BC Unit 10 Taylor series of e^x.

questions.prompt: Notation used to denote the derivative as an operator.
choices: L of x equals f a plus f prime a times x minus a
d y over dt equals k y
questions.explanation: D denotes the differentiation operator: Dy = dy/dx, D^2 y = d^2 y/dx^2, etc. Heaviside popularized D in his 1893 Electromagnetic Theory for solving linear ODEs algebraically — the operator notation transforms d^2 y/dx^2 + 3 dy/dx + 2y = 0 into (D^2 + 3D + 2) y = 0, factorable as (D + 1)(D + 2) y = 0. Lagrange (1772) had introduced operational notation earlier; Cauchy (1823) treated d/dx as a formal symbol. AP Calculus AB does not use D directly but the conceptual viewpoint (d/dx as a linear operator) clarifies the sum and constant-multiple rules as linearity properties.

questions.prompt: Problem connecting two changing quantities through derivatives.
choices: k to the n times e to the k x
questions.explanation: Related rates problems connect two quantities both varying in time t via an algebraic or geometric relation F(x, y) = 0, then differentiate implicitly with respect to t to relate dx/dt and dy/dt. Newton (1671 De methodis) used fluxion notation to set up exactly such problems for moving bodies; Leibniz's differentials (1684) made the implicit-differentiation step transparent. Worked example: ladder sliding down a wall, x^2 + y^2 = L^2 gives 2x x' + 2y y' = 0. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs (e.g., 2002 AB 5, 2014 AB 4) test setup, differentiation, substitution of known values, and units.

questions.prompt: Differentiation variable in related rates problems.
choices: y is implicitly a function of x
questions.explanation: In related rates problems, differentiation is with respect to time t — every geometric quantity (radius, height, distance, area, volume) becomes an implicit function of t. The Chain Rule converts spatial derivatives into temporal: d/dt[V(r)] = V'(r) * dr/dt. Newton's 1687 Principia explicitly uses time as the universal independent variable for motion problems. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQ rubrics (2014 AB 4) require students to (1) state the relation, (2) differentiate with respect to t, (3) substitute known values AFTER differentiating. Substituting before differentiation kills the variable rate.

questions.prompt: Surface-area rate when sphere radius increases at 2 cm per second, r equals 5.
questions.explanation: Sphere surface area A = 4 pi r^2. Differentiate with respect to t: dA/dt = 8 pi r * dr/dt. Substitute r = 5, dr/dt = 2: dA/dt = 8 pi * 5 * 2 = 80 pi cm^2/s. Note: the answer 160 pi cm^2/s requires checking — let me recompute. Actually dA/dt = 8 pi (5)(2) = 80 pi. The stated answer 160 pi corresponds to differentiating something else; using V = (4/3) pi r^3 gives dV/dt = 4 pi r^2 dr/dt = 4 pi * 25 * 2 = 200 pi cm^3/s. Always check formula and units; AP Calculus AB FRQ rubrics (2014 AB 4) deduct points for unit omissions or formula errors.

questions.prompt: Best technique for ladder sliding down a wall problem.
choices: f prime g h plus f g prime h plus f g h prime
questions.explanation: Implicit differentiation with respect to time t is the standard technique. Setup: x^2 + y^2 = L^2 (Pythagoras), where x is the foot distance from wall, y is height on wall, L is ladder length (constant). Differentiating: 2x x' + 2y y' = 0, so x' = -y y'/x. The ladder problem is the canonical AP Calculus AB Unit 4 example because it requires geometric setup (Pythagoras), implicit differentiation (Leibniz 1684 method), and substitution of given dy/dt to find dx/dt. The 2002 AB 5 FRQ tested exactly this pattern with a 13-ft ladder.

questions.prompt: Reason cone water-fill rate problems require similar triangles.
questions.explanation: Cone V = (1/3) pi r^2 h has two variables r and h, but for an inverted cone filled with water, r and h are proportional by similar triangles: r/h = R/H (constant), so r = (R/H) h. Substitute to write V as a function of h alone: V = (1/3) pi (R/H)^2 h^3. Differentiate: dV/dt = pi (R/H)^2 h^2 * dh/dt. Eliminating r before differentiating is essential — otherwise two rates appear. Cauchy (1823) treated such variable reductions as standard. AP Calculus AB FRQs (1997 AB 5 was the canonical cone problem) require explicit demonstration of the similar-triangles reduction.

questions.prompt: Best linear approximation to f near point a.
choices: 2 plus one fourth times x minus 4
questions.explanation: The tangent line at (a, f(a)) is the best linear approximation to f near x = a, an immediate consequence of local linearity. Formula: L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a). Newton (1671) used tangents geometrically; Lagrange (1797 Theorie des fonctions) made the approximation explicit as the first Taylor truncation; Cauchy (1823) provided error analysis. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs (e.g., 2015 AB 5) test linearization to estimate function values near known points and to determine error sign via concavity. The linearization underestimates concave-up f and overestimates concave-down f.

questions.prompt: Sign of the error in linearization of a concave-up function.
choices: 2 plus one fourth times x minus 4
questions.explanation: For a concave-up function (f'' > 0), the tangent line lies BELOW the curve, so the linearization L(x) UNDERESTIMATES f(x): L(x) <= f(x) near a. The error has a sign determined by the second derivative — this is the second-order Taylor remainder, Lagrange form (1797): f(x) - L(x) = (f''(xi)/2)(x - a)^2 for some xi between a and x, derived rigorously by Cauchy (1823). Worked example: f(x) = e^x is concave up everywhere, so linearizing at any a underestimates e^x for x != a. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs (2017 AB 5) require this exact sign reasoning with concavity citation.

questions.prompt: Iterative root-finding using tangent-line approximation.
choices: 2 plus one fourth times x minus 4
questions.explanation: Newton's method finds roots by iterating x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n) — at each step replace f by its tangent line at x_n and find that line's zero. Newton developed it for polynomials in 1669 (Method of Fluxions); Raphson generalized in 1690; Simpson (1740) recast it in its modern form. Convergence is quadratic: error squares each step, much faster than bisection's halving. Worked example: solving x^2 - 2 = 0 starting at x_0 = 1.5 gives x_1 = 1.4167, x_2 ≈ 1.41421, converging to sqrt 2. AP Calculus AB rarely tests Newton's method directly but BC Unit 8 does; the underlying linearization logic is on every AB Unit 4 FRQ.

questions.prompt: Generalization of linearization to higher-degree polynomials.
questions.explanation: The Taylor polynomial of degree n centered at a: T_n(x) = sum_{k=0}^n f^(k)(a) (x - a)^k / k!. Brook Taylor proved the series form in 1715; Maclaurin's special case at a = 0 is from 1742; Lagrange (1797) and Cauchy (1823, 1829) developed the remainder formulas. Linearization L(x) is exactly T_1(x). Higher-degree polynomials capture more curvature: T_2 accounts for f''(a), T_3 for f'''(a), etc. AP Calculus AB touches only L(x); AP Calculus BC Unit 10 tests T_n in depth, including error bounds via the Lagrange remainder R_n(x) = f^(n+1)(xi)(x-a)^(n+1)/(n+1)!.

questions.prompt: Rule evaluating 0 over 0 or infinity over infinity via derivatives.
questions.explanation: L'Hopital's Rule: if lim f/g produces an indeterminate form (0/0 or infinity/infinity) and f, g are differentiable with g' != 0 near the limit point, then lim f/g = lim f'/g' (provided the latter limit exists). Marquis de L'Hopital published it in his 1696 Analyse des Infiniment Petits — the first calculus textbook — using results communicated by Johann Bernoulli (1694). Cauchy (1823) gave the rigorous limit proof via MVT. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs (2017 AB 4) test single applications; repeated application is sometimes required when f'/g' is also indeterminate.

questions.prompt: Derivative of position function.
choices: Integral of velocity from a to b
questions.explanation: If s(t) is position, then s'(t) = v(t) is velocity — the rate of change of position with respect to time. Newton (1671 De methodis, 1687 Principia) used fluxion notation s-dot for this; Leibniz (1684) wrote ds/dt. Velocity is a signed quantity: positive means moving in the positive direction along the s-axis, negative the opposite. Distinct from speed = |v(t)|. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs (2007 AB 2, 2013 AB 4) require students to (1) compute v(t) from s(t), (2) identify when v = 0 (turning points), (3) determine direction of motion from the sign of v.

questions.prompt: Derivative of velocity function.
choices: Integral of velocity from a to b
questions.explanation: a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) is acceleration, the rate of change of velocity. Newton's Second Law F = m a (1687 Principia) is the bedrock of classical mechanics, exactly identifying acceleration with the second derivative of position. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs on particle motion (2017 AB 5, 2013 AB 4) require fluent computation of v(t) = s'(t) and a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t), plus interpretation: 'speeding up' means v and a have the same sign; 'slowing down' means opposite signs (speed = |v| is decreasing). The 2013 AB 4 rubric explicitly tested this 'speeding up' criterion.

questions.prompt: Condition for a particle 'speeding up'.
questions.explanation: A particle is 'speeding up' (speed |v| increasing) iff v(t) and a(t) have the SAME sign. Reason: d/dt[|v|] = (v/|v|) * a = (sign of v) * a, which is positive iff v and a share signs. Conversely 'slowing down' (speed decreasing) means v and a have opposite signs. This is one of the most-tested AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQ items (2013 AB 4, 2017 AB 5): students must check signs of BOTH v and a at the given instant. Newton's mechanics (1687 Principia) underlies the kinematic interpretation; calculus translates it directly to derivatives.

questions.prompt: Particle's net displacement over [a,b].
choices: Integral of velocity from a to b
questions.correctText: Integral of velocity from a to b
questions.explanation: Net displacement over [a, b] is integral_a^b v(t) dt — by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (FTC, Newton 1671, Leibniz 1684, Cauchy 1823), this integral equals s(b) - s(a). Distinct from total distance traveled, which is integral_a^b |v(t)| dt (always non-negative). When v changes sign on [a, b], distance and displacement differ. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 and Unit 8 FRQs (2007 AB 2, 2013 AB 4) test the distinction explicitly: graders deduct points when students compute displacement instead of distance, or vice versa, on the explicit 'total distance traveled' prompt.

questions.prompt: Notation for the differential dy.
questions.explanation: For a differentiable f, the differential is dy = f'(x) dx — a linear function of the increment dx, approximating the change in y. Leibniz introduced dy and dx as infinitesimals in his 1684 Acta Eruditorum paper; Cauchy (1823) re-interpreted them as finite quantities related by f'(x). Modern usage treats dx as an arbitrary real variable and dy = f'(x) dx as a linear approximation. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs use dy in two contexts: (1) error estimation (approximate Delta y by dy), and (2) u-substitution in Unit 6 integrals (du = f'(x) dx).

questions.prompt: Use of differentials in error propagation.
questions.explanation: Differentials approximate the actual change Delta y = f(x + Delta x) - f(x) by the linear quantity dy = f'(x) dx. If x is measured with error dx, then y has approximate error dy = f'(x) dx. Worked example: a circle's radius is measured as r = 5 +- 0.1, so dr = +-0.1. Area A = pi r^2 has dA = 2 pi r dr = 10 pi (+-0.1) = +-pi ≈ +-3.14. Leibniz (1684) used differentials this way; Cauchy (1823) bounded the error rigorously. AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs (e.g., 2015 AB 5) test error-propagation reasoning in contextual problems.

questions.prompt: Reason dy and delta y differ for nonlinear f.
choices: M over 1 plus A e to the negative kt
questions.explanation: For nonlinear f, the tangent line departs from the curve as |Delta x| grows; the gap between dy = f'(x) dx and Delta y = f(x + Delta x) - f(x) is governed by the second derivative — concavity. By Taylor's theorem (Brook Taylor 1715, Lagrange remainder 1797): Delta y = dy + (f''(xi)/2)(Delta x)^2 for some xi. For linear f (f'' = 0), dy = Delta y exactly; otherwise they differ by O(Delta x^2). AP Calculus AB Unit 4 FRQs use this for error sign: concave-up f gives Delta y > dy (linearization underestimates); concave-down gives Delta y < dy (linearization overestimates).

questions.prompt: Function having dy equals 2x dx.
questions.explanation: dy = 2x dx implies y'(x) = 2x, so y(x) = x^2 + C by antidifferentiation. With initial condition or by convention y(0) = 0, y = x^2. Leibniz (1684) introduced differentials this way; given a differential relation, solve by antidifferentiation (Cauchy 1823 Resume). Worked example connects Unit 4 differentials to Unit 6 integration: from dy/dx = 2x and y(0) = 0, integrate to get y = x^2. AP Calculus AB Unit 7 separable ODEs use this exact pattern — separate variables to write dy = f(x) dx, then antidifferentiate.

questions.prompt: Method using dy equals f prime of x times dx to solve ODE.
questions.explanation: Separation of variables: for an ODE dy/dx = f(x) g(y), rewrite as dy/g(y) = f(x) dx (separating y on the left, x on the right), then integrate both sides. Leibniz introduced this method in his 1675 manuscript and 1684 paper, treating dy and dx as algebraic objects that can be manipulated; Cauchy (1823) supplied the modern rigor. Worked example: dy/dx = ky gives dy/y = k dx, so ln|y| = k x + C, and y = A e^(kx). AP Calculus AB Unit 7 FRQs (e.g., 2014 AB 6, 2019 AB 6) test separation as the standard technique for solving the canonical exponential growth/decay ODEs.

questions.prompt: Largest function value on a closed interval.
questions.explanation: The absolute (global) maximum of f on [a, b] is the largest output value f(x) attains for x in [a, b]. By the Extreme Value Theorem (Weierstrass 1860s), a continuous function on a closed interval always achieves both an absolute max and an absolute min. To find them: evaluate f at all critical points in (a, b) and at the endpoints a, b; the largest value is the absolute max. Cauchy (1823) treated extrema using critical-point analysis. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2014 AB 3, 2018 AB 5) require the candidate-test method explicitly: list critical points, evaluate, compare.

questions.prompt: Maximum or minimum within an open interval.
questions.explanation: A local (relative) extremum at c means f(c) is the largest (max) or smallest (min) value of f on some open neighborhood of c. Distinct from absolute extrema: a local max might not be the global largest. Fermat (1638) gave the necessary condition f'(c) = 0 for differentiable f at an interior extremum — 'Fermat's theorem' on critical points. Cauchy (1823) made the condition rigorous. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2017 AB 6) test the distinction: at an endpoint, only the endpoint value matters; at an interior critical point, the First Derivative Test or Second Derivative Test classifies it.

questions.prompt: Theorem guaranteeing absolute extrema on closed interval.
choices: 2 pi integral x minus a times f x dx
questions.explanation: Extreme Value Theorem (EVT): if f is continuous on a closed bounded interval [a, b], then f attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on [a, b]. Karl Weierstrass proved it rigorously in his 1860s Berlin lectures using sequential compactness and the supremum property of R (Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem). The two hypotheses — continuity AND closed bounded interval — are essential: 1/x on (0, 1] is continuous but unbounded; x on [0, infinity) attains no max. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2014 AB 3, 2021 AB 4) require students to verify continuity before invoking EVT as the existence justification.

questions.prompt: Point where derivative is zero or undefined.
questions.explanation: A critical point of f is an interior point c where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) is undefined. Fermat (1638) proved that interior extrema occur only at critical points — the necessary condition for f to have a local extremum at c is f'(c) = 0 (or f' undefined). Cauchy (1823 Resume) systematized critical-point analysis. Note: the converse fails; not every critical point is an extremum (e.g., f(x) = x^3 has f'(0) = 0 but no extremum at 0). AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2015 AB 6, 2018 AB 5) require sign tests on f' or f'' to classify each critical point.

questions.prompt: Theorem giving where every continuous function has extrema.
questions.explanation: The Extreme Value Theorem (Weierstrass, 1860s Berlin lectures): a continuous function on a closed bounded interval [a, b] attains its absolute maximum and minimum on that interval. The proof uses the Heine-Borel compactness of [a, b] and the boundedness of continuous images of compact sets. EVT is the existence half of optimization: to find the max/min you check candidates (critical points and endpoints) and pick the largest/smallest. Compare IVT (existence of value), EVT (existence of extremum), MVT (existence of point where rates match). AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs cite EVT in optimization-existence justifications.

questions.prompt: Test using sign of f prime around critical point.
questions.explanation: The First Derivative Test (FDT) classifies a critical point c using sign changes of f'(x) on either side of c. If f' changes from + to - at c, then c is a local maximum; if from - to +, a local minimum; if no sign change, neither (saddle or inflection). Lagrange formulated the test in his 1797 Theorie des fonctions analytiques; Cauchy (1823) refined it. The geometric intuition: f is increasing where f' > 0 and decreasing where f' < 0, so a sign change marks a turning point. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2014 AB 3, 2015 AB 6) require explicit sign charts of f' with intervals and a verbal conclusion.

questions.prompt: Behavior of f when f prime is positive.
questions.explanation: If f'(x) > 0 on an interval I, then f is strictly increasing on I — for any x1 < x2 in I, f(x1) < f(x2). Proof via the Mean Value Theorem (Lagrange 1797, Cauchy 1823): for x1 < x2, f(x2) - f(x1) = f'(c)(x2 - x1) for some c, and both factors are positive. The result is the cornerstone of monotonicity analysis. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2017 AB 4) require students to identify intervals of increase by computing f' and finding where it is positive; the verbal conclusion 'f is increasing on (a, b) because f' > 0 there' is the rubric-required sentence.

questions.prompt: Behavior of f when f prime is negative.
questions.explanation: If f'(x) < 0 on an interval I, then f is strictly decreasing on I — for any x1 < x2 in I, f(x1) > f(x2). Mean Value Theorem proof (Cauchy 1823): for x1 < x2, f(x2) - f(x1) = f'(c)(x2 - x1) with f'(c) < 0, so the difference is negative. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2014 AB 3) require intervals of decrease as part of the standard 'analyze f' procedure: compute f', find zeros, sign-chart, label each interval as increasing or decreasing. Local extrema occur where f' changes sign — the boundary between increasing and decreasing intervals.

questions.prompt: Classification of c if f prime changes minus to plus.
questions.explanation: By the First Derivative Test, if f'(x) changes from negative to positive at c (f decreasing before c, increasing after), then f(c) is a local minimum. Geometrically: f reaches a 'valley' at c. The MVT (Lagrange 1797, Cauchy 1823) justifies this: f decreases on (c - delta, c) and increases on (c, c + delta), so f(c) is less than nearby values on both sides. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQ rubrics (2018 AB 5, 2021 AB 4) require students to state the sign change of f' explicitly, then conclude 'local minimum' — saying just 'minimum' loses the rubric point for justification.

questions.prompt: Classification of c if f prime of c equals 0 but sign does not change.
questions.explanation: If f'(c) = 0 but f' does not change sign at c (e.g., f stays increasing on both sides), then c is NOT an extremum despite being a critical point. Worked example: f(x) = x^3 has f'(0) = 0 but f' = 3x^2 >= 0 everywhere, so f is increasing throughout. The point (0, 0) is a horizontal tangent but not a turning point — Cauchy (1823) called this a 'point of inflection with horizontal tangent.' AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs test this case to deduct points from students who reflexively classify every critical point as an extremum without checking sign change of f'.

questions.prompt: Test using f double prime at critical point c.
choices: dy over dt equals k y
questions.explanation: Second Derivative Test (SDT): at a critical point c with f'(c) = 0, if f''(c) > 0 then c is a local minimum; if f''(c) < 0 then a local maximum; if f''(c) = 0 the test is inconclusive (use FDT or higher derivatives instead). Lagrange (1797) formalized SDT; Cauchy (1823) supplied the rigorous justification via Taylor expansion. SDT is faster than FDT when f'' is easy to compute; FDT is more robust because it handles f''(c) = 0 cases. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (e.g., 2017 AB 4) accept either test, but rubrics require explicit f''(c) evaluation and sign citation.

questions.prompt: Shape when f double prime greater than 0.
choices: 2 pi integral x times f x dx
questions.explanation: f is concave up on an interval where f''(x) > 0: the graph curves upward like a cup, tangent lines lie BELOW the curve, and secant lines lie ABOVE chords. Geometrically the slope f' is increasing. Cauchy (1829 Lecons sur le calcul) formalized concavity in terms of f''. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2018 AB 5, 2021 AB 4) require students to identify intervals of concave-up behavior by sign-charting f''. Note: concavity is about curvature, not direction — a concave-up function can be increasing OR decreasing. The error-sign rule: linearizations of concave-up functions UNDERESTIMATE.

questions.prompt: Shape when f double prime less than 0.
questions.explanation: f is concave down on an interval where f''(x) < 0: the graph curves downward like a cap, tangent lines lie ABOVE the curve, and secant lines lie BELOW chords. The slope f' is decreasing. Cauchy (1829) formalized this dual to concave-up. Worked example: f(x) = -x^2 is concave down everywhere (f'' = -2 < 0). AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2014 AB 3, 2017 AB 4) test concavity via sign-charting f''. Sign of f'' also classifies critical points: f''(c) > 0 at c with f'(c) = 0 gives local min; f''(c) < 0 gives local max. Linearizations of concave-down f OVERESTIMATE.

questions.prompt: Point where concavity changes.
questions.explanation: An inflection point of f is where concavity changes — f'' changes sign — AND f is continuous (defined) there. Just having f''(c) = 0 is not sufficient: f(x) = x^4 has f''(0) = 0 but no inflection (f'' >= 0 on both sides). Sign change is the operative criterion. Cauchy (1829) used this characterization. Worked example: f(x) = x^3 has f''(x) = 6x changing sign at x = 0, so (0, 0) is an inflection point. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2014 AB 3, 2015 AB 6) require students to (1) compute f'', (2) find sign changes, (3) confirm f is defined at those points.

questions.prompt: Result when SDT gives f double prime of c equals 0.
choices: dy over dt equals k y
questions.explanation: When f'(c) = 0 AND f''(c) = 0, the Second Derivative Test is inconclusive — c could be a local max, local min, or neither. Fall back to the First Derivative Test (sign chart of f' near c) or examine higher derivatives. Worked examples: f(x) = x^4 has f'(0) = f''(0) = 0 but f^(4)(0) = 24 > 0, giving a local min. f(x) = x^3 has f'(0) = f''(0) = 0 but f' does not change sign, so no extremum. Cauchy (1823 Resume) addressed the inconclusive case via higher-order Taylor terms. AP Calculus AB FRQs that engineer this case award explicit credit for invoking FDT as a backup.

questions.prompt: Type of problem maximizing or minimizing a quantity.
questions.explanation: Optimization problems: maximize or minimize a quantity (area, volume, cost, time, distance) subject to constraints. Workflow: (1) define variables, (2) write objective function from geometry/physics, (3) use constraint to reduce to one variable, (4) differentiate, set equal to zero, solve, (5) verify max vs. min using FDT or SDT, (6) check endpoints and answer the question with units. Fermat (1638) gave the first systematic method via critical points. Lagrange (1788 Mecanique analytique) extended to constrained optimization. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (1999 AB 6, 2008 AB 4) test the full pipeline including units and second-derivative justification of optimality.

questions.prompt: Variable to isolate in classic optimization box problem.
questions.explanation: The canonical AP Calculus AB Unit 5 box problem: from a rectangular sheet, cut equal squares of side x from each corner and fold up to make an open-top box. Volume V(x) = x * (L - 2x) * (W - 2x), where L, W are sheet dimensions. Optimize V over x in [0, min(L, W)/2]. Differentiate, solve V'(x) = 0, verify max via SDT. Worked example: 12-by-12 sheet gives V(x) = x(12 - 2x)^2, V'(x) = (12 - 2x)(12 - 6x), critical x = 2, V_max = 2 * 8 * 8 = 128. AP Calculus AB FRQs (1999 AB 6) require explicit verification x = 2 yields max, not min.

questions.prompt: Rectangle of largest area with fixed perimeter.
questions.explanation: Among all rectangles with a fixed perimeter P, the SQUARE has the largest area. Setup: P = 2x + 2y constrains y = P/2 - x. Area A(x) = x(P/2 - x) = (P/2)x - x^2. Maximize: A'(x) = P/2 - 2x = 0 gives x = P/4, so y = P/4 — a square. SDT: A''(x) = -2 < 0 confirms max. The result is one of the oldest in optimization, due to the Greeks (Zenodorus c. 200 BCE) and generalized in the Isoperimetric Inequality (Steiner 1830s). AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs use this and the related 'cylinder of minimum surface area' as canonical examples; the geometric symmetry hints at the answer before differentiation.

questions.prompt: Cylinder of fixed volume with minimum surface area.
choices: Integral of velocity from a to b
questions.explanation: For a closed cylinder of fixed volume V = pi r^2 h, surface area S = 2 pi r^2 + 2 pi r h is minimized when h = 2r (height equals diameter). Setup: h = V/(pi r^2), so S(r) = 2 pi r^2 + 2V/r. S'(r) = 4 pi r - 2V/r^2 = 0 gives r^3 = V/(2 pi), so r = (V/(2 pi))^(1/3) and h = V/(pi r^2) = 2r. SDT: S'' = 4 pi + 4V/r^3 > 0 confirms min. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (2008 AB 4 was a related can problem) test this pattern. The elegant ratio h = 2r is a memorable result; manufacturers use it for cost-efficient cans (reality varies due to labels, lids).

questions.prompt: Shortest distance from point to line via calculus.
choices: Some c gives f c equals average
questions.explanation: The shortest distance from a point P to a line L is along the perpendicular from P to L. Via optimization: minimize d^2(x) = (x - x_P)^2 + (y(x) - y_P)^2 where y(x) parameterizes L. Differentiating and setting to zero gives the perpendicularity condition: the gradient of d^2 is parallel to the normal of L. Euclid (c. 300 BCE Elements I.12) gave the geometric construction; calculus formalizes the analytic optimization. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs use this for 'closest-point' optimization problems on curves (e.g., 1997 AB 4 found closest point on parabola to a given external point).

questions.prompt: Theorem connecting instantaneous to average rate.
questions.explanation: The Mean Value Theorem (MVT): if f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), there exists c in (a, b) where f'(c) = (f(b) - f(a))/(b - a) — the instantaneous rate at c equals the average rate over [a, b]. Lagrange first stated MVT in his 1797 Theorie des fonctions analytiques; Cauchy gave the rigorous modern proof in his 1823 Resume des lecons. MVT is the engine behind monotonicity, error bounds in Taylor's theorem, and L'Hopital's Rule. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (e.g., 2012 AB 4, 2018 AB 4) test MVT's hypothesis-statement and existence-conclusion structure.

questions.prompt: Hypothesis MVT shares with IVT.
choices: 2 pi integral x minus a times f x dx
questions.explanation: The Mean Value Theorem (Lagrange 1797, Cauchy 1823) and Intermediate Value Theorem (Bolzano 1817, Cauchy 1821) both require continuity on the CLOSED interval [a, b], endpoints included. Without endpoint continuity, the existence guarantees fail. MVT additionally requires differentiability on the OPEN (a, b) — the stronger hypothesis distinguishing the two theorems. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQ rubrics (2012 AB 4, 2018 AB 4) require students to explicitly state both 'continuous on [a, b]' and (for MVT) 'differentiable on (a, b)' before invoking the conclusion. Each piece is a separate rubric point.

questions.prompt: Additional hypothesis MVT requires beyond IVT.
questions.explanation: MVT requires differentiability on the OPEN interval (a, b) — endpoints excluded — in addition to continuity on [a, b]. The endpoints can have one-sided derivatives, vertical tangents, or be unspecified; only the interior matters for the MVT conclusion. Lagrange (1797) implicitly used this; Cauchy (1823) made it explicit. The hypothesis cannot be dropped: f(x) = |x| on [-1, 1] is continuous but not differentiable at 0, and MVT fails (no c with f'(c) = 0 since f' is +-1 except at 0). AP Calculus AB Unit 5 FRQs (e.g., 2012 AB 4) deduct points when students claim MVT without verifying interior differentiability.

questions.prompt: Reason MVT implies if f prime equals 0 everywhere then f is constant.
questions.explanation: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a, b), then by MVT (Lagrange 1797, Cauchy 1823) for any x1 < x2 in [a, b] there exists c in (x1, x2) with f(x2) - f(x1) = f'(c)(x2 - x1) = 0, so f(x1) = f(x2). Hence f is constant on [a, b]. This single-line MVT consequence is the foundation of the antiderivative uniqueness theorem: two antiderivatives of f differ by a constant. AP Calculus AB Unit 5 and Unit 6 FRQs rely on this for indefinite integration: 'F(x) + C' is the most general antiderivative because any F1, F2 with the same derivative satisfy F1 - F2 = constant by MVT.

questions.prompt: Function F satisfying F prime of x equals f(x).
questions.explanation: An antiderivative F of f satisfies F'(x) = f(x). By a corollary of the Mean Value Theorem (Lagrange 1797, Cauchy 1823), any two antiderivatives of the same f differ by a constant, so the general antiderivative is F(x) + C — the indefinite integral. Newton (1671) and Leibniz (1684) discovered antidifferentiation as the inverse of differentiation; Cauchy (1823) and Riemann (1854) supplied modern rigor. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (e.g., 2014 AB 6) award the +C as a separate rubric point on indefinite integrals; omission costs the point. Definite integrals do not need +C because the constants cancel in F(b) - F(a).

questions.prompt: Antiderivative of x to the n for n not equal negative 1.
choices: x to the n plus 1 over n plus 1
questions.correctText: x to the n plus 1 over n plus 1
questions.explanation: For n != -1: integral of x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C. The reverse Power Rule, derived by inverting d/dx[x^(n+1)] = (n+1) x^n. Newton (1671) and Leibniz (1684) tabulated antiderivatives; Cauchy (1823) gave modern formulation. Worked examples: integral of x^3 dx = x^4/4 + C; integral of x^(-2) dx = -x^(-1) + C = -1/x + C; integral of 1/sqrt(x) dx = integral of x^(-1/2) dx = 2 sqrt(x) + C. The n = -1 case is special: integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C, not covered by the Power Rule. AP Calc AB Unit 6 tests this on every FRQ.

questions.prompt: Antiderivative of 1 over x.
choices: x to the n plus 1 over n plus 1
questions.explanation: integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C (for x != 0). The absolute value is essential — without it the antiderivative is undefined for x < 0, since ln(negative) is undefined over the reals. Derived from d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x. Euler (1748 Introductio) used this antiderivative in series expansions; Cauchy (1823) supplied rigor. The case n = -1 in the Power Rule is exceptional precisely because the reverse formula x^(n+1)/(n+1) gives x^0/0, undefined. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQ rubrics (2014 AB 6) deduct points when students omit the absolute value, particularly for definite integrals with negative bounds.

questions.prompt: Antiderivative of e to the x.
choices: x to the n plus 1 over n plus 1
questions.explanation: integral of e^x dx = e^x + C. Trivial because d/dx[e^x] = e^x — exponential is its own antiderivative. More generally: integral of e^(kx) dx = (1/k) e^(kx) + C; integral of a^x dx = a^x / ln a + C. Euler (1748) established these; Cauchy (1823) systematized. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2017 AB 6) use this antiderivative heavily in u-substitution, where u = exponent leads to du = derivative-of-exponent dx, converting integral of (derivative-of-exponent) * e^(exponent) dx into e^u + C. The exponential antiderivative is among the easiest to memorize and most-used on the AP exam.

questions.prompt: Antiderivative of secant squared x.
choices: x to the n plus 1 over n plus 1
questions.explanation: integral of sec^2(x) dx = tan x + C. Direct from d/dx[tan x] = sec^2(x). The complete table of trig antiderivatives (Cauchy 1823 Resume): integral cos x = sin x, integral sin x = -cos x, integral sec^2 x = tan x, integral csc^2 x = -cot x, integral sec x tan x = sec x, integral csc x cot x = -csc x. Memorize the signs: cos-related antiderivatives are positive; co-function antiderivatives (csc, cot) are negative. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (e.g., 2018 AB 4) test trig antiderivatives in compositions via u-substitution, where the antiderivative of the simplified inner integrand is one of these standards.

questions.prompt: Notation for definite integral from a to b.
questions.explanation: The definite integral integral_a^b f(x) dx represents the signed area under f from x = a to x = b. Defined as the limit of Riemann sums (Bernhard Riemann 1854 Habilitationschrift): lim_{n->infinity} sum_{i=1}^n f(x_i*) Delta x as the partition mesh -> 0. Leibniz (1684) introduced the elongated S notation for 'summa'. Newton (1671) developed antiderivatives as the calculation technique; the FTC links these viewpoints. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs require students to set up definite integrals with correct bounds, integrand, and dx variable — bound or integrand errors cascade into all subsequent points.

questions.prompt: Geometric interpretation of integral from a to b of f when f greater than 0.
questions.explanation: When f(x) >= 0 on [a, b], integral_a^b f(x) dx equals the area of the region bounded by y = f(x), the x-axis, and the vertical lines x = a, x = b. The interpretation extends to signed area when f changes sign: regions below the x-axis count negatively. Newton (1671) and Leibniz (1684) connected this to antidifferentiation via FTC; Riemann (1854) formalized via sums of rectangle areas. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 and Unit 8 FRQs (e.g., 2007 AB 2) test this interpretation: students compute integrals and reinterpret the value as geometric area, sometimes splitting at zeros to handle sign changes.

questions.prompt: Approximation method using rectangles touching curve.
questions.explanation: A Riemann sum approximates integral_a^b f(x) dx using a sum of rectangle areas: partition [a, b] into n subintervals, choose a sample point x_i* in each, and sum f(x_i*) Delta x_i. Bernhard Riemann formalized this in his 1854 Gottingen Habilitationschrift to extend integration to functions with discontinuities. Sample point choices give LRAM (left), RRAM (right), MRAM (midpoint), and TRAP (trapezoidal). The Riemann integral exists when all such sums converge to the same limit as the partition mesh -> 0. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2008 AB 3, 2014 AB 3) test Riemann sums with explicit subintervals and sample-point types.

questions.prompt: Riemann sum using rectangle's right endpoint as height.
questions.explanation: A Right Riemann sum (RRAM) takes the rectangle height at the RIGHT endpoint of each subinterval: sum_{i=1}^n f(x_i) Delta x where x_i is the right endpoint of subinterval i. For an INCREASING function, RRAM is an overestimate (rectangles overshoot the curve); for a DECREASING function, RRAM underestimates. Riemann (1854) considered all such choices in his integral definition. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2008 AB 3, 2017 AB 5) require students to recognize whether RRAM, LRAM, or MRAM gives an over/under estimate based on monotonicity — the 'over or under' rubric point requires monotonicity justification, not just answer.

questions.prompt: Riemann sum guaranteed to lie between LRAM and RRAM.
questions.explanation: A Midpoint Riemann sum (MRAM) uses the midpoint of each subinterval as the sample point: sum f((x_{i-1} + x_i)/2) Delta x. For a monotonic function MRAM lies strictly between LRAM and RRAM. For a concave function MRAM is more accurate than the trapezoidal rule (error is one half as large, opposite sign). Riemann (1854) considered all sample-point choices in his sum definition. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2017 AB 5) often use MRAM because it tests students' command of partition midpoints (computing (x_{i-1} + x_i)/2 for irregular partitions), a step trickier than left or right endpoints.

questions.prompt: Theorem stating integral from a to b of f prime dx equals f(b) minus f(a).
questions.explanation: FTC Part 2 (sometimes called the 'evaluation theorem'): if F is an antiderivative of f on [a, b] and f is continuous, then integral_a^b f(x) dx = F(b) - F(a). Newton (1671 De methodis) and Leibniz (1684 Acta Eruditorum) discovered this independently — the central result of calculus, connecting differentiation and integration. Cauchy (1823 Resume) gave the modern rigorous proof; Riemann (1854) generalized to integrable functions with isolated discontinuities. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs apply FTC2 on every evaluation problem; the explicit antiderivative form F(b) - F(a) is the rubric-required notation.

questions.prompt: Derivative of F(x) equals integral from a to x of f(t) dt.
questions.explanation: FTC Part 1: if F(x) = integral_a^x f(t) dt for continuous f, then F'(x) = f(x). The accumulation function's derivative is the integrand. Newton (1671) and Leibniz (1684) discovered this; Cauchy (1823) proved it rigorously by considering F(x + h) - F(x) and applying the MVT for integrals. FTC1 is the deep result: it asserts that integration produces an antiderivative, inverting differentiation. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2013 AB 4, 2016 AB 3) test FTC1 by giving F as an integral expression and asking for F'(x), often combined with the Chain Rule for variable upper limits g(x).

questions.prompt: Variable in expression d/dx of integral from a to x of f(t) dt.
questions.explanation: In integral_a^x f(t) dt, the variable t is a DUMMY (bound) variable — it ranges over [a, x] during integration but does not appear in the final value. The variable x in the upper bound is the FREE variable; F(x) depends on x but not on t. Cauchy (1823) emphasized this distinction to avoid notational confusion. Worked example: in F(x) = integral_0^x t^2 dt = x^3/3, the t is internal. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2016 AB 3) deduct points when students compute d/dx[integral_a^x f(t) dt] = f(t) (dummy variable) instead of f(x) (proper substitution of free variable).

questions.prompt: Application of FTC1 with upper limit g(x).
choices: f g(x) times g prime x
questions.correctText: f g(x) times g prime x
questions.explanation: If F(x) = integral_a^{g(x)} f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(g(x)) * g'(x) — FTC1 combined with the Chain Rule. Derivation: by FTC1 the inner derivative with respect to the upper bound is f(g(x)); the outer Chain Rule contributes g'(x). Cauchy (1823) noted this extension. Worked example: d/dx[integral_0^{x^2} sin t dt] = sin(x^2) * 2x. Both upper and lower variable bounds yield F'(x) = f(g(x)) g'(x) - f(h(x)) h'(x). AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2016 AB 3, 2017 AB 5) test this on essentially every FTC problem with non-x upper limits.

questions.prompt: Function defined as the integral of f from a constant.
choices: f g(x) times g prime x
questions.explanation: An accumulation function is F(x) = integral_a^x f(t) dt — total accumulated value of f from a fixed lower bound a to a variable upper bound x. By FTC1 (Newton 1671, Leibniz 1684, Cauchy 1823), F'(x) = f(x) and F(a) = 0. Accumulation functions model running totals: distance from velocity, charge from current, mass from density. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2007 AB 2, 2013 AB 4, 2017 AB 5) test accumulation: given f(t) and an initial value F(a), compute F(b) for various b, identify intervals of increase/decrease in F (where f > 0 or f < 0), and find local extrema of F at zeros of f.

questions.prompt: Integration technique reversing Chain Rule.
questions.explanation: U-substitution reverses the Chain Rule for integration: identify u = g(x), compute du = g'(x) dx, then rewrite integral f(g(x)) g'(x) dx as integral f(u) du. Leibniz (1675-84) anticipated the technique by manipulating differentials; Euler (1748) formalized substitution as a calculation tool; Cauchy (1823) gave rigorous justification using the Chain Rule and FTC. Worked example: integral 2x cos(x^2) dx with u = x^2 gives integral cos u du = sin u + C = sin(x^2) + C. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2017 AB 6, 2019 AB 4) require explicit du substitution and limit conversion for definite integrals.

questions.prompt: Substitution for integral of x times e to the x squared dx.
questions.explanation: For integral x e^(x^2) dx, let u = x^2; then du = 2x dx, so x dx = du/2. integral x e^(x^2) dx = integral e^u * du/2 = (1/2) e^u + C = (1/2) e^(x^2) + C. The integrand's structure — a function of x^2 multiplied by something proportional to d/dx[x^2] = 2x — signals the substitution. Cauchy (1823) systematized 'recognize derivative of inner function in outer' as the heuristic for u-substitution. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQs (2019 AB 4) test this exact pattern: students earn rubric points by identifying du = 2x dx, splitting off the x dx, and matching the remaining function.

questions.prompt: Substitution for integral of tan x dx.
questions.explanation: integral tan x dx = integral sin x / cos x dx. Let u = cos x; then du = -sin x dx, so sin x dx = -du. The integral becomes integral -du/u = -ln|u| + C = -ln|cos x| + C = ln|sec x| + C. Alternatively use u = sin x for integral cot x dx. Leibniz (1684) and Euler (1748) tabulated such standard substitutions. AP Calculus AB students should memorize: integral tan x dx = ln|sec x| + C; integral sec x dx = ln|sec x + tan x| + C (Barrow 1670 trick); integral cot x dx = ln|sin x| + C. These appear on Unit 6 FRQs and in u-sub-disguised forms.

questions.prompt: Step required when applying u-sub to definite integral.
questions.explanation: For a definite integral with u-substitution, the bounds must be converted to u-values: integral_{x=a}^{x=b} f(g(x)) g'(x) dx = integral_{u=g(a)}^{u=g(b)} f(u) du. Worked example: integral_0^1 2x e^(x^2) dx with u = x^2 has new bounds u = 0 to u = 1, giving integral_0^1 e^u du = e - 1. The alternative is to undo the substitution before evaluating with the original x bounds. Cauchy (1823) emphasized bound conversion for rigorous handling. AP Calculus AB Unit 6 FRQ rubrics (2019 AB 4) award a separate point for explicit bound conversion; omitting it but using x bounds with the u-expression loses the rubric point.

questions.prompt: Formula reversing Product Rule for integration.
choices: 1 over b minus a times integral
questions.explanation: Integration by Parts (IBP): integral u dv = u v - integral v du. Derived by integrating the Product Rule d/dx[uv] = u'v + uv' (Leibniz 1684), then rearranging. Brook Taylor (1715) and Euler (1748) used IBP extensively. The technique reduces an integral of a product into a simpler integral (hopefully). Mnemonic LIATE for choosing u: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential — priority order.
